era_context: The early Italian Renaissance; Ming China under the Xuande Emperor; the Hundred Years' War in decline; the Ottoman Empire consolidating Balkan territories; the Council of Constance's aftermath reshaping European Christianity.

events.title: Zheng He's sixth treasure voyage returns to Nanjing
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's sixth treasure voyage fleet returns to Nanjing after departing in 1433 (chronologically, the sixth voyage occurred before the seventh). The massive treasure ships had visited East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and Indian ports, demonstrating Ming naval supremacy and diplomatic reach. These voyages represent the apex of Chinese maritime exploration before the Ming court's shift toward isolationism in the late 1430s.

sources.name: Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan (Overall Survey of the Star Raft)

events.certainty_note: The exact dates of Zheng He's voyages are debated among scholars. The sixth voyage is traditionally dated 1421–1422 or 1433–1435, depending on the source. This entry reflects uncertainty in the historical record.

events.title: Joan of Arc captured at Compiègne
events.description: Joan of Arc, the French military leader who rallied French forces during the Hundred Years' War, is captured by Burgundian forces at Compiègne on May 23, 1431 (not 1432; this entry corrects a potential confusion). By 1432, Joan is imprisoned and awaiting trial. Her capture marks a turning point in the Hundred Years' War, as French military momentum is disrupted. Joan's subsequent trial and execution in 1431 would make her a symbol of French national identity and Catholic martyrdom.

sources.name: Trial records of Joan of Arc

events.certainty_note: Joan of Arc's capture date is May 23, 1431, not 1432. Her trial and execution occurred in 1431.

events.title: Hussite Wars continue in Bohemia
events.description: The Hussite Wars (1419–1434), a series of religious and political conflicts in Bohemia following Jan Hus's execution in 1415, continue in 1432. The radical Taborite faction is being defeated by a coalition of moderate Hussites and Catholic forces. By 1434–1435, the wars will effectively conclude with a settlement granting the Utraquist church recognition within the Holy Roman Empire. The wars represent one of the earliest major religious conflicts in Christian Europe and establish Bohemia as a center of religious reform.
key_figures: Sigismund (Holy Roman Emperor, King of Bohemia)
events.certainty_note: The Hussite Wars' progression is well-documented in contemporary records.

events.title: Portuguese exploration of West African coast continues
events.description: Under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator, Portuguese caravels continue systematic exploration of the West African coast. By 1432, Portuguese traders have established contacts with Berber and sub-Saharan African merchants, initiating the Atlantic slave trade (initially small-scale) and seeking gold, spices, and geographic knowledge. These voyages represent the beginning of European maritime expansion and the eventual circumnavigation of Africa.

sources.name: Portuguese royal chronicles and navigational records
Later accounts by Gomes Eanes de Zurara (Crónica da Guiné)

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration in 1432 is attested in later chronicles; contemporary documentation is sparse.
cross_references: 1444-001 (First large-scale slave raid from West Africa)
1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

events.title: Florentine Renaissance patronage expands
events.description: In Florence, the early Renaissance is flourishing. By 1432, major artistic and architectural projects are underway, including Brunelleschi's dome of the Florence Cathedral (completed 1436) and the early works of Donatello and Masaccio. The Medici family, though not yet dominant, is beginning to emerge as a major patron of the arts. The cultural efflorescence of Florence in the 1430s–1440s will establish the city as the intellectual and artistic capital of the Renaissance.

sources.name: Giorgio Vasari, Lives of the Artists

events.certainty_note: The artistic and architectural projects of Florence in 1432 are well-documented in contemporary records and later accounts.

disconfirming_evidence: Joan of Arc's capture (1432-002) occurred in 1431, not 1432; her trial and execution also occurred in 1431. The dating of Zheng He's voyages (1432-001) is uncertain; some sources place the sixth voyage in 1421–1422, others in 1433–1435. Portuguese exploration (1432-004) is often portrayed as a unified program under Henry the Navigator, but early voyages were sporadic and motivated by diverse commercial and political interests.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1432 is moderate. European political and military events are well-attested in chronicles and official records. Chinese court records are rich for Zheng He's voyages, though dating is uncertain. Portuguese exploration is documented in later chronicles but contemporary records are sparse. African perspectives on Portuguese contact are almost entirely absent from surviving sources, reflecting the colonial bias of European documentation. The year 1432 is significant primarily as a waypoint in larger processes (the Hussite Wars' conclusion, the treasure voyages' continuation, the Florentine Renaissance) rather than as a year of singular events.

graph_edges.note: Sixth voyage return precedes the seventh voyage departure
Joan of Arc's capture in 1431 leads to her trial and execution
Hussite Wars continue toward their conclusion
